mission.title: Install and Configure Pipecd on Kubernetes
mission.description: PipeCD is a GitOps style continuous delivery platform that provides a consistent deployment and operations experience for any applications across multiple cloud environments. Installing PipeCD allows you to streamline your deployment processes and enhance your operational efficiency.

steps.title: Add the PipeCD Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the PipeCD Helm repository to your local Helm installation with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add pipecd https://charts.pipecd.dev/
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install PipeCD using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install PipeCD in a new namespace called 'pipecd' with the following command:
```bash
helm install pipecd pipecd/pipecd --namespace pipecd --create-namespace --version v0.55.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the PipeCD pods are running successfully with:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace pipecd
```

steps.title: Port-forward the PipeCD API server
steps.description:
To access the PipeCD API server, run the following command:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/pipecd-api --namespace pipecd 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of PipeCD will show all pods running in the 'pipecd' namespace. You can access the PipeCD API server at http://localhost:8080 after port-forwarding. Additionally, the resource limits should be configured as specified.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add pipecd https://charts.pipecd.dev/
helm repo update
helm install pipecd pipecd/pipecd --namespace pipecd --create-namespace --version v0.55.0

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the PipeCD installation, first uninstall the Helm release with the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall pipecd --namespace pipecd
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volumes that were created during the installation:
```bash
kubectl delete crd pipelineruns.pipecd.dev
kubectl delete crd applications.pipecd.dev
kubectl delete crd environments.pipecd.dev
kubectl delete crd deployments.pipecd.dev
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the 'pipecd' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace pipecd
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of your PipeCD installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace pipecd -o yaml > pipecd-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to apply the latest version of PipeCD:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade pipecd pipecd/pipecd --namespace pipecd --version v0.56.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the PipeCD pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace pipecd
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice that the PipeCD pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for more information:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace pipecd
```
Look for any error messages that might indicate what is causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: API server not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you are unable to access the PipeCD API server, ensure that the port-forwarding command is running. You can check the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace pipecd
```
If the service is not running, try restarting the port-forward command.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing pod eviction
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are being evicted due to resource limits, check the events for the namespace:
```bash
kubectl get events --namespace pipecd
```
You may need to adjust the resource limits in your deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during the Helm upgrade, ensure that you are using a compatible version of the chart. Check the available versions with:
```bash
helm search repo pipecd/pipecd --versions
```
Then, specify a valid version in your upgrade command.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
